findings.message: Source redirected to different domain: consent.yahoo.com

content_preview.title: d-Matrix Moves Its Corsair Inference Accelerator Into Full Production, Betting On-Chip SRAM Beats the GPU at Decode
content_preview.summary: d-Matrix says its Corsair inference accelerator is in full production, pairing on-chip SRAM with GPUs to cut AI response times by more than 10x.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The AI-chip startup d-Matrix announced on June 9, 2026 that its Corsair inference accelerator platform is in full production, with hardware set to ship in volume to priority hyperscalers, neoclouds, and frontier labs, according to the company's release [distributed through PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/d-matrix-corsair-ai-inference-platform-enters-full-production-to-meet-customer-demand-302795218.html). Rather than chase Nvidia in raw training throughput, d-M...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (689 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clean Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis arc. Every specific traces to a cited source, and the article is careful to attribute the headline 10x figure to the company and to disclose that the underlying benchmark is a single third-party test by Gimlet Labs.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all five gzipped snapshots in sources/2026-06/d-matrix-moves-its-corsair-inference-accelerator-into-full-production-betting-on-chip-sram-beats-the-gpu-at-decode/. source-0 (PR Newswire, 200): CONFIRMS full production, the prefill/decode split, the verbatim 'GPUs dominate the compute-intensive prefill portion... while Corsair excels at the decode phase' quote, 'speed up AI model response time by more than 10x', the Gimlet Labs 24-second-to-under-two-seconds benchmark, Sid Sheth's 'Age of AI Inference' and 'Corsair takes off from where the GPU leaves off' quotes verbatim, the June 9 2026 date, TSMC + Alchip manufacturing, the Arista/Broadcom/Supermicro SquadRack ecosystem, and the hyperscalers/neoclouds/frontier-labs customer categories. source-2 (SiliconANGLE, 200): CONFIRMS 'embeds processing components into the memory', 'repurposed some of Corsair's SRAM circuits to carry out vector-matrix multiplications', the $275M Series C at a $2B valuation led by Bullhound/Triatomic/Temasek, '9,600 trillion calculations per second' on MXINT4, and the SquadRack 100-billion-parameter-in-SRAM claim. source-4 (The Register, 200): CONFIRMS the per-card 2GB SRAM @ 150 TB/s, 256GB LPDDR5 @ 400 GB/s, the 'blisteringly fast SRAM... much slower, but higher capacity LPDDR5' framing, and 2.4 PFLOPS (MXINT8) / 9.6 PFLOPS (MXINT4). source-1 and source-3 (both finance.yahoo.com) bot-blocked: their snapshots captured the consent.yahoo.com GDPR/privacy consent wall (Italian-locale 'Le tue scelte relative alla privacy', ~2.7KB, no article body), not the article. source-1's facts (TSMC, Alchip, Arista, Broadcom, Supermicro) are independently confirmed verbatim in the PR Newswire release (source-0). For source-3, as a documented last resort I performed a single live WebFetch of the cited allowlisted Yahoo URL, which confirmed verbatim: Microsoft participates via its M12 venture arm; Qatar Investment Authority and Singapore's Economic Development Board Investments joined as new participants; and 'tenfold better performance, triple the cost efficiency, and up to five times superior energy efficiency compared to GPU-based systems.'
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No hallucinations detected. Every direct quote appears verbatim in its cited snapshot. No fabricated dual-card aggregate specs (no 6,400 mm2 / 4 GB / 300 TB/s / 4,800 TFLOPs) — the article correctly uses The Register's per-card figures. No CNBC-only specifics leaked in (no TSMC 6nm node detail, no ~$500M figure, no 'DRAM chokepoint' phrasing); the article instead uses TSMC N6 only where PR Newswire supports it. Entity names ('d-Matrix', 'Corsair', 'M12') reproduced verbatim.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Performance claims are consistently framed as the company's own or as a single third-party benchmark, and the 'What We Don't Know' section explicitly flags the absence of named customers and standardized benchmarks. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Original. No prior d-Matrix or Corsair coverage in the archive (the lone 'corsair' grep hit is an unrelated Saronic warship article). Closest adjacent coverage is the June 10 Intel Crescent Island piece and the Feb 8 Positron AI piece — both inference accelerators betting on LPDDR over HBM — which makes this a complementary, not duplicative, story. Curated related_articles added to the meta file.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Technically valid (signature and hash verified, all 18 checks pass). All five sources personally read; three load-bearing snapshots (PR Newswire, SiliconANGLE, The Register) fully corroborate every specific and quote. The two Yahoo consent-wall snapshots are bot-blocks, not content failures, and their facts are corroborated independently (source-1 via PR Newswire; source-3 via a documented last-resort live WebFetch of the cited allowlisted URL). Ready to publish as-is.
